q: What is a techfin and why is their name the reverse of fintech?

options.A: A regulated bank that acquires technology start-ups to add digital financial products.
options.B: A fintech start-up that pivots to licensing its software to incumbent banks.
options.C: A technology platform that offers financial services second, being a tech company first.
options.D: A financial firm that builds its own technology platform to disrupt Silicon Valley.

q: Why did Alibaba create Alipay and how did this business grow over time? How is Alipay related to Ant Financial, later known as Ant Group?

options.A: It was created to offer high-interest money market funds, later merging into Tencent.
options.B: It was created in 2004 to build buyer-seller trust via escrow, later spun into Ant Financial.
options.C: It was launched in 2009 as a mobile-only wallet, then renamed WeChat Pay.
options.D: It began as a credit scoring service and grew into the Zhima Credit platform.

q: How did the instant messaging and gaming company Tencent move into financial services? What was the first financial product that it launched?

options.A: It added payments to messaging, first launching TenPay in 2005 to counter Alipay.
options.B: It first launched the money market fund Licaitong in 2014 to rival Yu'ebao.
options.C: It first launched WeBank, a challenger bank, offering Weilidai microloans.
options.D: It first launched WeChat Pay via the 2005 Red Envelope viral campaign.

q: What is a bigtech and how do they compare in size to the incumbent financial institutions such as banks?

options.A: Chinese platforms like Alibaba and Tencent that are smaller than state-owned banks.
options.B: Global banks that acquired technology firms, matching VISA and JPMorgan in size.
options.C: North American firms whose market value is comparable to but below ICBC's.
options.D: North American tech firms whose market capitalization far exceeds the largest banks.

q: What has been Amazon's path into financial services? What financial products does it offer and why?

options.A: It partnered with Goldman Sachs to launch a credit card and savings account.
options.B: It built payments from 2007, expanding by trial-and-error to support its marketplace.
options.C: It relied on external partnerships to launch a full deposit-holding bank.
options.D: It began with merchant loans in 2007, later adding a stablecoin for transfers.

q: How has Apple found success in embedding financial services into its iOS ecosystem?

options.A: By partnering with incumbents to embed banking and protect its smartphone share.
options.B: By building financial products internally to monetize customers for profit.
options.C: By addressing institutional voids in the underbanked US smartphone market.
options.D: By launching a stablecoin backed by fiat currencies over its ecosystem.

q: What was Facebook's Libra project and what happened to it?

options.A: A digital wallet backed by major banks that succeeded after regulatory approval.
options.B: A money market fund modeled on Yu'ebao that was folded into Facebook Pay.
options.C: A stablecoin backed by fiat and US Treasuries; regulators killed it, rebranded Diem, shut down.
options.D: A peer-to-peer payment service over Messenger that scaled globally by 2022.

q: What was the experience of Google with its payment product? How did it evolve over time?

options.A: It quickly overtook Apple Pay in mobile wallet transaction volume by 2020.
options.B: It succeeded with Plex bank accounts launched with Citigroup in 2021.
options.C: It avoided relaunches, keeping a single stable Google Wallet product since 2011.
options.D: It struggled with product-market fit, relaunching repeatedly and losing ground to rivals.

q: What are the three competitive strengths of the techfins and bigtech in financial services?

options.A: Deposit insurance subsidies, branch networks, and risk management expertise.
options.B: Platform ecosystems and network effects, customer access and brand, and big data insights.
options.C: Regulatory compliance, escrow accounts, and low-cost stable funding sources.
options.D: Interchange fees, credit scoring systems, and bricks-and-mortar proximity.

q: How serious is the threat from techfins and bigtech for incumbent financial institutions? Will banks, insurance companies, and asset managers become obsolete?

options.A: No; entry has had mixed success, and the winning combination is bigtech-bank partnerships.
options.B: Yes; bigtech will replace banks as they move well beyond payments into full banking.
options.C: Yes; Chinese techfins have already disintermediated domestic banks entirely.
options.D: No; because regulators have banned techfins and bigtech from all financial services.
